project.name: Sublemonable
project.description: Decentralized, zero-knowledge, end-to-end encrypted ephemeral messaging. Identity anchored on Algorand Layer 2. Messages routed through a permissionless P2P relay network. No central server.
project.migration_strategy: v1 users migrate by linking their existing account to an Algorand identity. All encryption, UI, and message behavior is identical to v1.

new_in_v2: packages/relay-client — P2P relay network client library
node/relay — relay node server software (run by community)
node/bootstrap — bootstrap node for initial peer discovery

removed: server/ — centralized Go server replaced by relay node network
Single PostgreSQL instance — replaced by distributed relay node storage
kept_identical: All UI components and design system (packages/ui) — unchanged
All app UX — web, iOS, Android — unchanged from user perspective
JWT auth — replaced by wallet signature auth (transparent to user)
All open source files, docs, contributing guides
changed: Identity registration — from centralized DB to Algorand smart contract
Prekey storage — from centralized server to relay node DHT
Message delivery — from single server WebSocket to relay node network
Authentication — from JWT to cryptographic wallet signature (secp256k1 or ed25519)
Account discovery — from UUID lookup to on-chain identity resolution
added: Sealed Sender — sender identity encrypted inside envelope, relay never knows sender
Message padding — all messages padded to 256-byte blocks before encryption
Panic / wipe mode — instant local data destruction gesture
Device verification — multi-device key registry signed by identity key
Memory zeroing — sensitive buffers zeroed immediately after use
Identity key rotation — users can rotate long-term keys with on-chain proof
Algorand identity layer — public keys anchored on-chain
Relay node network — permissionless, anyone can run a node
Node staking — relay nodes stake ALGO to deter spam and sybil attacks
Bootstrap nodes — initial peer discovery run by Sublemonable team

sealed_sender.description: Sender identity is encrypted inside the message envelope. The relay network only knows the recipient — never the sender.
sealed_sender.implementation: libsignal SealedSender API — available in libsignal-client for all platforms
sealed_sender.metadata_protected: Even a compromised relay node cannot determine who sent a message to whom
sealed_sender.note: This is the single highest-value security addition from v1 to v2

message_padding.description: All messages padded to fixed-size blocks before encryption to prevent traffic analysis based on message length
message_padding.implementation: Append PKCS7-style padding to plaintext before passing to Signal Protocol encrypt
message_padding.cost: Negligible — tiny bandwidth overhead, zero UX impact

panic_mode.description: Instant full wipe of all local data, keys, and messages
triggers: Dedicated panic gesture — 3-finger long press on main screen
Duress PIN — secondary PIN that looks like normal unlock but silently wipes
Remote wipe — send a special encrypted signal from another linked device
what_is_wiped: All session keys and ratchet state
All identity and prekeys from local storage

os_specific.android: deleteRecursively() on app data dir + Keystore key deletion
os_specific.ios: SecItemDelete all Keychain entries + wipe app documents dir

panic_mode.after_wipe: App appears as freshly installed — no evidence of prior use

device_verification.description: Each linked device has its own keypair signed by the user's identity key. Contacts can see and verify all linked devices.
device_verification.binding: Device public key signed by identity key, signature stored on-chain
device_verification.new_device_flow: New device shows as unverified to contacts until user confirms via safety number comparison
device_verification.revocation: User can revoke any device key — revocation recorded on-chain

memory_protection.description: Sensitive data zeroed from memory immediately after use

implementations.go_relay_node: Use crypto/subtle.ConstantTimeCompare and explicit zeroing with defer
implementations.typescript_web: Uint8Array.fill(0) on all key material immediately after use
implementations.ios: Use SecureEnclave for identity key — key never enters app memory
implementations.android: Android Keystore hardware-backed — key operations happen in secure element

memory_protection.swap_prevention: Keys marked with mlock equivalent where OS supports it

identity_key_rotation.description: Users can rotate their long-term identity key if they believe it is compromised
identity_key_rotation.on_chain: New identity key published to Algorand contract, signed by old key as proof of continuity
identity_key_rotation.contact_notification: All contacts receive encrypted notification of key change via relay network
identity_key_rotation.contact_action: Contacts prompted to re-verify safety number
identity_key_rotation.old_key: Marked as rotated on-chain with timestamp — old sessions invalidated

traffic_obfuscation.description: Optional transport layer that makes Sublemonable traffic indistinguishable from generic HTTPS
traffic_obfuscation.note: For high-risk users in network surveillance environments

algorand_layer.purpose: Decentralized identity registry and relay node coordination. Messages NEVER touch the blockchain.

network.testnet: testnet-v1.0 — used during development and v2 beta

layer_2.solution: Algorand State Channels or AVM-native smart contracts
layer_2.note: Pure L1 Algorand is fast enough (~3.5s finality, ~0.001 ALGO per tx) that a full L2 may be unnecessary. Use L1 for identity ops which are infrequent. Evaluate L2 only if volume demands it.

token.rationale: No Sublemonable token. Using native ALGO avoids tokenomics complexity, regulatory risk, and speculation. Users just need a small amount of ALGO for tx fees (~0.1 ALGO to register).

IdentityRegistry.purpose: Maps user account IDs to their public identity keys on-chain
methods: register(identity_key, signed_prekey, signature) — opt-in, costs ~0.1 ALGO
deregister() — marks account inactive, clears keys

RelayRegistry.purpose: Directory of active relay nodes. Nodes register here to be discoverable.

local_per_relay: endpoint: bytes — WSS URL of relay node
public_key: bytes[32] — relay node's signing key

methods: register_node(endpoint, public_key) — requires min stake of 10 ALGO
heartbeat() — relay calls this every 5 minutes to signal liveness

local_per_account: device_keys: bytes[] — up to 5 device public keys
device_signatures: bytes[] — each signed by identity key

wallet_integration.web: Pera Wallet Connect or WalletConnect v2 — user signs txs in their wallet app
wallet_integration.note: For most users, wallet interaction is invisible — keys are managed by Sublemonable. Advanced users can bring their own Algorand wallet.

relay_network.architecture: Permissionless P2P relay network. Anyone can run a node. Nodes store encrypted message envelopes temporarily until delivery.

message_flow.step_1: Sender looks up recipient prekey bundle from Algorand IdentityRegistry
message_flow.step_2: Sender performs X3DH key exchange using prekey bundle
message_flow.step_3: Sender encrypts message with Signal Protocol (Double Ratchet)
message_flow.step_4: Sender wraps in Sealed Sender envelope — relay cannot identify sender
message_flow.step_5: Sender pads ciphertext to 256-byte block boundary
message_flow.step_6: Sender queries RelayRegistry on-chain for active relay nodes
message_flow.step_7: Sender connects to 2-3 relay nodes and submits encrypted envelope
message_flow.step_8: Relay nodes store envelope (max 72h) until recipient connects
message_flow.step_9: Recipient connects to relay, fetches envelope, sends delivery ACK
message_flow.step_10: Relay deletes envelope immediately on ACK
message_flow.step_11: Recipient decrypts — Sealed Sender reveals true sender only to recipient

what_relay_knows: Approximate message size (padded to 256-byte blocks, so only block count)
Rough timing of submission and delivery

storage.engine: BadgerDB (embedded key-value, no external DB dependency)

anti_spam.proof_of_work: Client must solve lightweight Hashcash puzzle before submitting envelope
anti_spam.rate_limiting: Max 100 envelopes per sender address per hour
anti_spam.stake_requirement: Relay nodes must stake 10 ALGO on-chain — slashable for misbehavior

relay_node.heartbeat: Node calls RelayRegistry.heartbeat() on Algorand every 5 minutes to signal liveness
relay_node.config_file: relay.toml — endpoint, Algorand node URL, stake wallet, storage path, TLS, Tor

bootstrap_nodes.purpose: Initial hardcoded relay nodes run by Sublemonable team to bootstrap the network
bootstrap_nodes.note: These are the nodes you run initially. As community nodes register on-chain, clients prefer community nodes.
bootstrap_nodes.decentralization_milestone: When 10+ independent nodes are active, bootstrap nodes step back to observer role

client_relay_library.purpose: Used by all app clients to interact with relay network
exports: discoverRelays() -> RelayNode[] — reads from Algorand RelayRegistry
client_relay_library.relay_selection: Client connects to 2 relays simultaneously for redundancy. Prefers low-latency nodes. Rotates relay selection periodically.
client_relay_library.fallback: If all discovered relays are unreachable, fall back to bootstrap nodes

initial_setup: 1. Provision 2 VPS instances (Hetzner CX21 or equivalent — ~$5/month each)
3. Run: docker run sublemonable/relay-node with relay.toml config
4. Fund an Algorand wallet with ~20 ALGO per node (stake + tx fees)
5. Register each node on RelayRegistry smart contract
6. Nodes appear in on-chain directory — clients discover automatically
7. Monitor via relay node dashboard (built into node/relay)
for_founder.scaling: Add more nodes as user count grows. Community nodes reduce your hosting costs over time.

requirements: A VPS with a public IP and domain
for_community_operators.incentives_v2: No token rewards in v2 — operators run nodes for ideological reasons or to support users in their region. Token incentive layer is a v3 consideration.

v1_to_v2_user_flow.step_1: App update prompts user to migrate to decentralized identity
v1_to_v2_user_flow.step_2: App generates Algorand key pair (or user connects existing wallet)
v1_to_v2_user_flow.step_3: App registers existing identity key to Algorand address via IdentityRegistry contract
v1_to_v2_user_flow.step_4: Small ALGO fee required (~0.1 ALGO) — app can optionally subsidize this for first N users
v1_to_v2_user_flow.step_5: Existing contacts are notified of on-chain identity via relay network
v1_to_v2_user_flow.step_6: v1 server kept running read-only for 90 days, then decommissioned
v1_to_v2_user_flow.note: From the user's perspective: one migration screen, one confirmation. All messages and contacts carry over.

migration.backwards_compatibility: v2 clients can communicate with v1 server during transition period via compatibility shim in packages/relay-client

build_order.prereq: v1 must be fully shipped and stable before starting v2

phase_1.name: Security Additions (can ship as v1.1)
deliverables: Sealed Sender — integrate libsignal SealedSender API across all platforms
Message padding — 256-byte block padding in packages/crypto
Panic mode — wipe gesture + duress PIN on all platforms
Memory zeroing — Uint8Array.fill(0) after key use in TypeScript, defer zeroing in Go
Device verification UI — show linked devices, allow revocation

phase_2.name: Algorand Contracts + Chain Package
deliverables: contracts/IdentityRegistry.py — write, test on testnet
contracts/RelayRegistry.py — write, test on testnet
contracts/DeviceRegistry.py — write, test on testnet
packages/chain — TypeScript Algorand client library
Deploy all contracts to Algorand testnet
Integration tests for all contract methods

phase_3.name: Relay Node Software
deliverables: node/relay — Go relay node with BadgerDB, WSS, anti-spam, heartbeat
packages/relay-client — TypeScript relay network client
node/bootstrap — bootstrap node config
Docker image for relay node
relay.toml config documentation
Founder runs 2 relay nodes on testnet

phase_4.name: App Integration
deliverables: Replace server calls with packages/relay-client in apps/web
Replace server calls with relay client in apps/ios
Replace server calls with relay client in apps/android
Algorand wallet integration in all apps
Migration flow UI — v1 to v2 onboarding screen
End-to-end integration tests on testnet

phase_5.name: Mainnet Launch
deliverables: Deploy contracts to Algorand mainnet
Founder runs 2 mainnet relay nodes
v2 app release
docs/NODE_OPERATION.md — community node setup guide
v1 server set to read-only, 90-day deprecation notice
AUDIT.md — commission third-party audit of contracts + relay node

relay_node_docker.auto_update: Watchtower container for automatic relay node updates

ci_cd.contracts: GitHub Actions — run PyTEAL compilation + Algorand sandbox tests on every PR
ci_cd.relay_node: GitHub Actions — Go tests + Docker build + push to ghcr.io on merge to main
ci_cd.security_scan: Semgrep + Trivy + smart contract audit tooling (Tealer for TEAL analysis)

monitoring.on_chain: Algorand indexer for contract state monitoring
monitoring.alerts: PagerDuty or similar for bootstrap node downtime

fable_instructions.note: v2 blueprint — build AFTER v1 is fully shipped. Read sublemonable-MASTER.json for v1 context first.
fable_instructions.start_here: Read both sublemonable-MASTER.json and this file completely before writing a single line of code
order: 1. Add Sealed Sender to packages/crypto — wrap libsignal SealedSender API
2. Add message padding (256-byte blocks) to packages/crypto encrypt/decrypt
3. Add panic mode to all three apps — wipe gesture, duress PIN, wipe implementation per platform
4. Add memory zeroing to all key operations in packages/crypto
5. Write contracts/IdentityRegistry.py in PyTEAL with ARC-4 ABI
6. Write contracts/RelayRegistry.py in PyTEAL with ARC-4 ABI
7. Write contracts/DeviceRegistry.py in PyTEAL with ARC-4 ABI
8. Write packages/chain — TypeScript Algorand client using algosdk
9. Write node/relay — Go relay node (BadgerDB, WSS, anti-spam, heartbeat, Tor option)
10. Write packages/relay-client — TypeScript relay network client
11. Update apps/web to use packages/relay-client instead of direct server calls
12. Update apps/ios to use relay client
13. Update apps/android to use relay client
14. Write migration flow UI for v1 to v2 transition
15. Write docker-compose.relay.yml and relay.toml example config
16. Write docs/NODE_OPERATION.md — how to run a community relay node
17. Write GitHub Actions workflow for contract testing (.github/workflows/contracts.yml)
18. Deploy and test all contracts on Algorand testnet
critical_rules: MESSAGES NEVER TOUCH THE BLOCKCHAIN — only identity keys and relay node registry go on-chain
Sealed Sender must be implemented before relay network — the relay must never know the sender
Message padding must happen BEFORE Sealed Sender wrapping — pad plaintext, then seal, then send
Panic mode wipe must be irreversible and fast — target under 2 seconds for full wipe
Relay nodes store ONLY encrypted blobs — no plaintext, no metadata, no sender information
All relay node storage uses BadgerDB TTL — no manual deletion logic, TTL handles expiry
Algorand transactions are signed client-side — relay node and server never hold wallet keys
Stake requirement on RelayRegistry prevents sybil attacks — do not remove this
Bootstrap nodes are fallback only — clients must prefer on-chain discovered community nodes
All v1 critical rules still apply — no logging, no plaintext storage, parameterized SQL etc
Memory zeroing is not optional — implement in every function that handles key material
Duress PIN must be indistinguishable from real PIN to an observer — same UI, same timing
algorand_specific: Use algosdk v2+ (TypeScript) — not the deprecated v1 API
All contracts use ARC-4 ABI for method routing
Test all contracts on testnet before mainnet — use Algorand sandbox for CI
Minimum ALGO balance for account registration is 0.1 ALGO — document this clearly for users
Use atomic transaction groups where multiple contract calls must succeed together
Contract state reads are free — only writes cost ALGO — optimize for read-heavy patterns
